agent-ops.body: Every new software category eventually rediscovers operations. Agent ops is the point at which the romance meets the incident review.

agent-ops.misuse: It gets abused when a tracing dashboard is mistaken for control. Visibility helps, but it does not solve brittle plans, weak tools, or the small issue of giving software enough access to embarrass the company at scale.
agent-ops.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means collecting run histories, replaying failures, scoring outcomes, tracking tool use, setting approvals, and deciding which errors deserve engineering rather than theology.

translations.label: platform meaning

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Making the agent's mistakes legible enough to fix them before they become policy.

agent-ops.warningLabel: Instrumentation is not the same thing as restraint.
agent-ops.whyExists: Agents create long traces, tool calls, permissions, side effects, retries, approvals, and costs. Ordinary application logs are rarely enough when the system can go wrong in six steps and a cheerful tone.

askNext: Who authored the skill, and can we audit the instructions and scripts?

agent-skills.body: Skills are a healthy idea when treated as maintainable operational assets. They become less healthy when installed like browser extensions and trusted like saints.
agent-skills.example: "The finance skill helped the agent produce the right spreadsheet; the security review then asked why everyone had installed it with the serenity of a gift basket."
agent-skills.misuse: They get treated as plugins, permissions, or magic competence capsules. A skill can guide behaviour and provide tools, but it still needs trust boundaries, review, versioning, and a system that knows when not to use it.
agent-skills.practicalMeaning: In practice it means packaging domain knowledge so the agent can discover the skill cheaply, load detailed guidance only when needed, and run deterministic helper scripts or consult reference files without bloating every task.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A reusable onboarding pack for the agent, loaded only when the work calls for it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A filesystem-shaped way to make agents more specialised without stuffing every task with every instruction.

agent-skills.warningLabel: A skill is capability packaging, not proof the agent is safe or competent.
agent-skills.whyExists: They exist because agents need domain procedures and repeatable workflows, but putting every possible instruction into the main context window is expensive, noisy, and a reliable way to teach the model nothing in particular.

agent-sprawl.body: Agent sprawl is not proof that the company is advanced. It is proof that procurement got easier than architecture.
agent-sprawl.example: "The company did not have an agent strategy so much as agent weather, with local outbreaks and confused reporting lines."
agent-sprawl.misuse: It gets used to sneer at any experimentation at all. Some multiplication is healthy. Sprawl begins when the overlap, risk, and maintenance burden outgrow the visibility and discipline around them.
agent-sprawl.practicalMeaning: In practice it means duplicate agents answering the same question differently, unclear ownership when one breaks, overlapping permissions, rising spend, inconsistent policy handling, and a governance meeting that discovers the estate only after the estate has discovered payroll.

translations.text: Innovation is thriving, and control has politely left the building.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality

agent-sprawl.whyExists: Agent tooling has become easier to launch than to govern. Local teams can spin up useful workflows quickly, and those small wins accumulate into a larger estate of permissions, prompts, budgets, and duplicated behaviours that nobody intended to administer as a single system.

agent2agent.body: Standards matter most when everyone is about to build the same messy thing in slightly incompatible ways.
agent2agent.example: "Agent2Agent made the handoff cleaner, which was useful, though it did not improve the quality of the thinking at either end."
agent2agent.misuse: It gets discussed as though inter-agent communication automatically creates competence, trust, or composability. It does not. A standard message shape cannot rescue a bad agent any more than a calendar invite can rescue a bad meeting.
agent2agent.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means one system can delegate a task to another specialised system without hard-coding every detail of the exchange. That can make ecosystems more flexible. It can also spread failure, ambiguity, and responsibility across a larger map.

agentic-ai.body:
The good question is not whether a system is agentic. The good question is whether the added autonomy meaningfully improves outcomes over a simpler, more inspectable design.

If the answer is "mainly in the pitch deck", you have your diagnosis.
agentic-ai.example: "Their agentic AI platform turned out to be a sensible approval workflow with retrieval, a model, and three places the system could politely stop pretending to know."
agentic-ai.misuse: It is abused whenever vendors want ordinary automation to sound frontier-adjacent. A brittle chain of prompts can be sold as agentic. So can a dashboard feature that merely triggers three API calls. The word now frequently signals aspiration more than architecture.
agentic-ai.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means workflow software wrapped around a model: planning prompts, tool calls, retries, validation, memory, approval checkpoints, and cost controls. The question is not whether it feels agentic. The question is whether it is useful, bounded, and worth the extra failure surface.

agi.body: AGI remains useful as a provocation and dangerous as a schedule. Once it enters a room, timelines harden, valuations inflate, and ordinary questions about reliability start dressing more modestly.
agi.example: "The deck mentioned AGI on slide three, which saved everyone the trouble of asking how the product planned to make money this year."
agi.misuse: By treating it as a launch category, a press strategy, or a vague aura that descends on any model after a particularly strong demo. AGI is a very large claim, not a mood.
agi.note: People use AGI to mean slightly different things: human-level performance on most cognitive tasks, economically transformative autonomy, or merely "the models got unsettlingly competent". The ambiguity is part of the term's power.
agi.practicalMeaning: In practice the term often functions as strategic theatre: it shapes fundraising, policy lobbying, hiring plans, and media attention long before anyone agrees on what threshold would actually count.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A future-facing claim that lets today's systems borrow tomorrow's mystique.

agi.warningLabel: Usually a forecast dressed as a noun.

ai-psychosis.body:
The phrase is useful only if it stays disciplined. It should point to distorted interpretation around AI, not become a lazy way to pathologise disagreement.

If a conversation needs clinical language, use clinical language. If it needs cultural criticism, use this term with that narrower ambition.
ai-psychosis.example: "After a week with the chatbot, he was speaking as if the product had opinions, trauma, and a five-year strategy. The rest of the team called it AI Psychosis and gently took the API keys away."
ai-psychosis.misuse: It gets abused as a cheap insult for anyone enthusiastic about AI, which makes it less useful. Genuine interest in the field is not the same thing as delusional over-interpretation. The phrase is most credible when it points to exaggerated belief formation, not mere excitement or scepticism.
ai-psychosis.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone has stopped evaluating systems by evidence and started treating outputs, roadmaps, or rumours as proof of exceptional intelligence, moral authority, or historical inevitability. It can show up as compulsive prompting, anthropomorphism, policy overreaction, or investment logic built on vibes.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone has stopped distinguishing between a persuasive interface and a reliable understanding of what the system is doing.

ai-psychosis.warningLabel: Use carefully. This is a cultural term, not a psychiatric diagnosis.
ai-psychosis.whyExists: People need a label for the strange social effect AI systems can have on users, founders, pundits, and investors: obsession, magical thinking, and the loss of proportion. The term exists because "you are taking autocomplete far too personally" was too long for social media.

ai-roll-up.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means some real workflow improvement, some software unification, some headcount pressure, and a great many founders discovering that regional service businesses have more edge cases than a prompt can politely absorb.

approval-flows.body: Approval flows belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.

approval-flows.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.

approval-flows.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.
approval-flows.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Tool Permissions are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.

askNext: What evidence does the approver actually see before they are asked to sign off?
Can the reviewer delay or block the action without needing executive theatre of their own?
If the approval disappeared, what real control would be lost?
approval-theatre.body: Approval theatre is what happens when the approval artefact matters more than the approval decision. The paperwork flourishes precisely as the control weakens.
approval-theatre.example: "The agent had three approval steps, two screenshot exports, and absolutely no stage at which anyone could still say no. Approval theatre was having a very productive quarter."
approval-theatre.misuse: Not every lightweight approval path is theatre. Fast approvals can still be real if the reviewer sees the right evidence and can meaningfully block the action. The phrase earns its keep when the approval is decorative, rushed, or structurally toothless.
approval-theatre.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means unread dashboards, one-click sign-offs, ritual thumbs-up messages, reviewers with no context, and nominal gates that cannot stop the system because the incentives, access, or deadlines have already decided the outcome.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The review happened loudly enough to be seen, but not strongly enough to change the outcome.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A gate designed to comfort observers more than to control the workflow.

approval-theatre.whyExists: Organisations genuinely need review and accountability. Under delivery pressure, though, it is temptingly easy to build approval surfaces that reassure executives, auditors, or product teams without giving reviewers enough evidence or authority to matter.

askNext: Is this about model quality, openness, or European strategic positioning?
Are we discussing a company, a market thesis, or a sovereignty argument?
Does the appeal come from capability, independence, or both?
arthur-mensch.body: When a founder becomes a stand-in for continental ambition, every product announcement begins carrying a diplomatic undertone.
arthur-mensch.example: "They cited Arthur less as a founder and more as a geopolitical exhibit for why Europe did not have to outsource all of its ambition."
arthur-mensch.misuse: By turning Mistral into either a romantic openness story or a simple anti-Big Tech morality play. The reality is more strategic: capability, optionality, regulation, distribution, and enterprise positioning are all in the room.
arthur-mensch.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Arthur Mensch, they usually mean the possibility that serious model-building can happen outside the dominant US lab pattern, and that sovereignty itself has become part of the AI argument.

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: A signal that AI strategy has acquired a map.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for Mistral's role in Europe's attempt to matter directly in model building, not merely in regulation.

arthur-mensch.whyExists: Because AI has a geography problem as well as a compute problem. Mensch's name often gets used as shorthand for European model ambition, strategic independence, and a less concentrated map of where frontier capability can come from.

askNext: What source material is the answer actually grounded in?
Is the convenience coming from better retrieval, or from hiding more of the raw map beneath chat?

ask-maps.body: Maps products have always wanted to be more than navigation. Ask Maps is simply the first version confident enough to speak in full sentences about it.
ask-maps.example: "Ask Maps was excellent at understanding the question, which left the older problem of trusting the answer exactly where it was."
ask-maps.misuse: It is described as local intelligence when it is still a synthesis layer built on listings, reviews, platform incentives, and model judgement. The interface becomes more humane. The uncertainty does not retire.
ask-maps.practicalMeaning: In practice it means conversational local search, quicker trip planning, reservation prompts, and one more surface where the user is encouraged to ask in prose while seeing less of the underlying source material directly.

translations.label: user meaning
translations.text: A way to ask the map the actual question instead of reverse-engineering it into filters.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Conversational local search with a friendlier interface and the same old need to verify the details.

ask-maps.whyExists: People do not think in tidy filter chips. They think in awkward constraints: chargers, queues, children, parking, weather, lighting, and whether the coffee is worth the detour. Ask Maps exists to make those messy requests legible to the product.

askNext: What new oversight or recovery burden appears if this workflow acts without immediate human review?
Which actions require stronger permissions, reversibility, or incident response once autonomy increases?
Is the expected gain large enough to justify the extra operational burden?
autonomy-tax.body: Autonomy tax is the part founders call friction and operators call Tuesday. The mature version of the discussion is deciding where the tax buys genuine leverage rather than resenting its existence.
autonomy-tax.example: "The demo made autonomy look cheap. The autonomy tax appeared later in the form of audit trails, retries, and a supervisor rota nobody had priced."
autonomy-tax.misuse: It gets used either as proof that autonomy is a foolish idea or as a temporary inconvenience that clever architecture will somehow abolish. Neither is serious. The tax can be worth paying, but somebody still has to pay it.
autonomy-tax.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means sandboxing, permission design, approval rules, rollback paths, richer logs, retry policy, exception handling, and humans who can intervene before the workflow turns initiative into a support ticket.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Every extra degree of freedom tends to purchase a matching degree of supervision.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The hidden cost of making the workflow less manual and more answerable after the fact.

autonomy-tax.whyExists: More autonomous systems can create more value, but they also create more surfaces on which something can go wrong without a human noticing in time. The term exists to name the operational overhead that arrives with that independence.
